title: GoThru 3D Floor Map API Go Project Report

issues.display_title: Issue #2: Task 1: Project Foundation and Data Models
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete — Task 1: Project Foundation and Data Models. Ready for Unit Testing

Summary

Backend foundation in place: GORM data models, a database connection layer, and automatic schema migration on startup. Verified against the live PostgreSQL instance — all tables created successfully.

Deliverables

File Purpose
src/models/models.go All data models + AllModels() for migration
src/config/database.go ConnectDB, buildDSN, InitDB (connect + AutoMigrate)
src/config/config.go Init() wires DB init into startup, clear fatal on failure
src/config/database_test.go Unit test: empty DSN returns a clear error
docs/schema.sql Reference DDL (PostgreSQL) matching the models
.gitignore, .env.example Ignore real .env; provide placeholder template

Data Model

Five tables (see schema deviation below):

  • users — account holder (name, email unique, password, email_verified)
  • projects — floormap project owned by a user
  • project_files — assets belonging to a project
  • otps — single-use tokens; type categorizes the flow (email_verification, reset_password, …)
  • subscriptions — user paid plan

Shared Base: UUID primary key (varchar(36)), created_at / updated_at, soft-delete deleted_at.

Schema Deviation (needs review)

The plan named six tables including separate email_verifications and password_reset_tokens. These were consolidated into a single otps table with a type discriminator (email_verification, reset_password, extensible). Result: 5 tables instead of 6. Rationale: one token mechanism, less duplication, easy to add new token types. Flagging for reviewer approval.

Acceptance Criteria

  • First run auto-creates all tables — db.AutoMigrate(models.AllModels()...) in InitDB; verified live (otps, project_files, projects, subscriptions, users)
  • Empty database address → clear error, no crash — ConnectDB("") / buildDSN return descriptive errors; covered by unit test
  • All required tables present — verified via information_schema query against live DB
  • Project references all required libraries — gorm, gorm/driver/postgres, gorm/driver/mysql, google/uuid, fiber/v2

Verification

  • go vet ./... — clean
  • go test ./... — ok (config), no failures
  • go build ./... — success
  • Live connect + AutoMigrate against PostgreSQL — all 5 app tables created
  • docs/schema.sql executed in a rollback transaction — valid

Notes for Reviewer

  1. Schema deviation — confirm the OTP consolidation (5 vs 6 tables) is acceptable.
  2. Security — the original .env (with real DB credentials) was committed in e355def. It is now gitignored, but remains in git history. Recommend rotating the DB password; optionally purge history.
  3. .env loading — the app reads os.Getenv only; .env is not auto-loaded. Consider adding godotenv (or rely on exported env vars in deployment).
  4. Field-level schema was inferred (plan docs were not accessible). Open to adjustment.

Task 1: Project Foundation and Data Models

Docs

2026-06-19-floormap-backend-overview.md
2026-06-19-floormap-backend-plan.md

Context

Before any feature can be built, the backend service needs a home to live in. Right now there is no Go project, no database connection, and no definition of what data the system stores. This task creates that foundation so every subsequent task has something solid to build on.

Goal

Set up the backend project structure and define every piece of data the system will store — users, projects, files, email tokens, password tokens, and subscriptions — as a connected database schema.

What We're Building

  • The project skeleton (the Go equivalent of "creating a new app") with all its third-party libraries declared
  • A complete data model: six tables that describe everything GoThru 3D stores about its users and their work
  • A database connection that automatically creates those tables when the service starts up
  • A basic test confirming that the connection fails gracefully when no database is configured

Why It Matters

Every other task in this plan reads from or writes to the database. If the data model is wrong or the connection is unreliable, bugs will ripple through the entire system. Getting this right first means the rest of the build is on a clean foundation.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Running the project for the first time automatically creates all six tables in the database — no manual SQL scripts needed
  • Attempting to connect with an empty database address returns a clear error instead of crashing
  • All six tables are present: users, projects, project files, email verifications, password reset tokens, subscriptions
  • The project correctly references all required libraries

What Changes After This Task

Every other task can now import the data models and database connection. The system has a place to store and retrieve data.

issues.display_title: Issue #3: Task 2: Safe Error Messages for Users
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete — Task 2: Safe Error Messages for Users Ready for Unit Testing

Summary

A single utility converts any internal error into a user-safe message before it reaches the caller — stripping SQL, query fragments, and stack traces.

Deliverables

File Purpose
src/utils/errors.go SafeErrorMessage(err error) string
src/utils/errors_test.go Unit tests covering every acceptance criterion

Behavior

func SafeErrorMessage(err error) string
  • PostgreSQL errors (*pgconn.PgError, found via errors.As so wrapped GORM errors still match): returns the database's human-readable Message only — no SQL statement, no query text.
  • Any other error: returns the first line of the message (drops multi-line stack traces), trimmed.
  • nil error → empty string.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Plain message ("connection refused") passes through unchanged
  • Multi-line message trimmed to the first line
  • Database constraint error returns the human-readable description without SQL text
  • No raw SQL, table names, or query fragments appear in the output (test asserts absence of INSERT/INTO/SELECT/query text)

Verification

  • go test ./src/utils/ — 3 tests pass (TDD: written failing first, then implemented)
  • go vet ./... — clean
  • go test ./... — ok, no failures
  • go build ./... — success

Notes for Reviewer

  • For Postgres, pgErr.Message may include the constraint name (e.g. ..."users_email_key"). This is the database's own human-readable text and contains no SQL/query. If table-name exposure inside constraint names must also be masked, the helper can be extended — flagging for decision.
  • Helper is ready for use by HTTP handlers from Task 6 onward.

Task 2: Safe Error Messages for Users

Docs

2026-06-19-floormap-backend-overview.md
2026-06-19-floormap-backend-plan.md

Context

When something goes wrong inside the backend — a database error, a constraint violation, an unexpected failure — the system needs to tell the user something went wrong without revealing the technical details of how the system works internally. Raw database error messages often contain table names, column names, or query fragments that expose system internals and can be a security risk.

Goal

Create a single utility that intercepts any internal error and converts it into a clean, user-safe message before it is sent back to the caller.

What We're Building

A small helper function that takes any error the backend encounters and returns a safe string:

  • For database-specific errors: returns the human-readable message the database provides (e.g. "duplicate key value violates unique constraint") without any SQL or query text
  • For all other errors: returns just the first line of the message, stripping any technical stack trace

Why It Matters

Security and professionalism. Showing a user a raw SQL error like ERROR: duplicate key value violates unique constraint "users_email_key" on table "users" (INSERT INTO users ...) is both a poor experience and a potential security leak. This helper ensures every error response is clean, consistent, and safe.

Acceptance Criteria

  • A plain error message ("connection refused") passes through unchanged
  • A multi-line error message is trimmed to just the first line
  • A database constraint error returns the human-readable description without any SQL text
  • No raw SQL, table names, or query fragments appear in any error response

What Changes After This Task

All HTTP handlers can now safely return error details to callers without worrying about exposing internal system information. This helper is used by every handler from Task 6 onwards.

issues.display_title: Issue #4: Task 3: Session Tokens and Password Security
issues.main_description_html:
Task 3: Session Tokens and Password Security

Docs

2026-06-19-floormap-backend-overview.md
2026-06-19-floormap-backend-plan.md

Context

Two fundamental security operations underpin user accounts: proving who a user is (session tokens) and protecting their password (hashing). These are not UI features — they are the security primitives the rest of the auth system is built on top of. They need to be correct, tested, and isolated before any login or register logic is written.

Goal

Build the three cryptographic helpers the auth system depends on: generating and verifying session tokens, generating and verifying one-time link tokens (for email verification and password reset), and securely hashing and checking passwords.

What We're Building

  • Session token (JWT): A signed, time-limited credential issued after login. Carried in every subsequent request to prove the user is signed in. Expires after 30 days.
  • One-time link token: A random secret used in email verification and password reset links. Stored only as a hash — the raw value goes in the email link and is never stored.
  • Password hashing: Passwords are never stored in plain text. They are irreversibly hashed before storage and compared safely at login time.

Why It Matters

These are the lowest-level building blocks of the auth system. If session tokens can be forged or passwords can be reversed, the entire platform is compromised. Getting this layer right — and testing it thoroughly — protects everything built on top of it.

Acceptance Criteria

  • A session token is issued and can be verified to recover the original user ID and email
  • A session token has exactly three parts separated by dots (the standard JWT format)
  • Two separately generated one-time tokens are never identical
  • The raw one-time token and its stored hash are always different
  • A correct password passes the check; an incorrect password is rejected
  • No password is ever stored in readable form

What Changes After This Task

The login, register, email verification, and password reset handlers can now be built. These helpers are the security foundation they all depend on.

RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete — Task 4: Automated Email Sending Ready for Unit Testing

Summary

A lightweight email service that triggers Plunk templates for transactional emails. Email design/copy stays in Plunk; the backend only triggers the right template with the recipient's name and link.

Deliverables

File Purpose
src/services/email.go EmailService + constructor + send methods
src/services/email_test.go Unit tests (httptest) covering every acceptance criterion
.env / .env.example Plunk config variables

Behavior

Constructor — NewEmailService() reads from the environment:

Variable Purpose
PLUNK_API_KEY Plunk secret API key (sent as Authorization: Bearer)
PLUNK_VERIFY_TEMPLATE event/template name for verification emails
PLUNK_RESET_TEMPLATE event/template name for password reset emails
PLUNK_BASE_URL optional, defaults to https://api.useplunk.com

Send methods

  • SendVerificationEmail(toEmail, name, link string) error — triggers the verify template.
  • SendPasswordResetEmail(toEmail, name, link string) error — triggers the reset template.

Both POST to Plunk /v1/track with { event, email, data: { name, link } }. An unconfigured template name returns a clear error; a non-2xx response surfaces the status.

Acceptance Criteria

  • Service builds and compiles without errors (go build ./...)
  • Verification template name comes from an environment variable (PLUNK_VERIFY_TEMPLATE), not hardcoded
  • Reset template name comes from a separate environment variable (PLUNK_RESET_TEMPLATE)
  • Service passes the user's name and full link URL to the email platform (asserted in tests via a capturing HTTP server)

Verification

  • go test ./src/services/ — 2 tests pass (TDD: failing first, then implemented). Tests use httptest to capture and assert the outgoing request (auth header, event name from env, email, data.name, data.link).
  • go vet ./... — clean
  • go test ./... — ok, no failures
  • go build ./... — success

Notes for Reviewer

  • Plunk integration uses the event trigger endpoint (/v1/track); the configured template name is sent as the event that drives the Plunk automation/template. Confirm this matches the Plunk setup (vs. the raw /v1/send transactional endpoint).
  • data payload carries name and link — confirm these match the variable names used inside the Plunk templates.
  • PLUNK_API_KEY must be set in deployment; left blank in local .env for now.

Task 4: Automated Email Sending

Docs

2026-06-19-floormap-backend-overview.md
2026-06-19-floormap-backend-plan.md

Context

GoThru needs to send two types of transactional emails to users: an email verification link after signup, and a password reset link when requested. These emails are not designed or written in code — their design and copy live in the Plunk email platform. The backend's only job is to trigger the right template with the right data.

Goal

Build the email-sending service that the auth handlers call when they need to send a verification or reset email.

What We're Building

A lightweight email service that:

  • Connects to the Plunk email platform using a secret API key stored in the environment
  • Sends a verification email by triggering the correct Plunk template and passing in the recipient's name and their unique verification link
  • Sends a password reset email by triggering a different Plunk template with the recipient's name and their unique reset link
  • Uses template names configured via environment variables — no email content is hardcoded

Why It Matters

Without this service, users who register cannot verify their email, and users who forget their password are locked out permanently. This is a required piece of the signup and recovery flows.

Acceptance Criteria

  • The service builds and compiles without errors
  • The template name used for verification emails comes from an environment variable, not hardcoded text
  • The template name used for reset emails comes from a separate environment variable
  • The service passes the user's name and the full link URL to the email platform

What Changes After This Task

The registration handler (Task 6) can send verification emails on signup. The forgot-password handler can send reset emails on request.

RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete — Task 5: Request Authentication Gate Ready for Unit Testing

Status: Implementation complete → moving to In Review
Date: 2026-06-22
Stack: Go 1.25.5 · Fiber v2 middleware · utils.VerifySessionToken (Task 3)

Summary

A single reusable authentication middleware that sits in front of protected routes: it verifies the session token, attaches the user's identity to the request, and rejects unauthenticated requests with 401 before any handler runs.

Deliverables

File Purpose
src/middleware/auth.go Auth() gate + UserID / UserEmail helpers
src/middleware/auth_test.go Unit tests (Fiber app.Test) covering every acceptance criterion

Behavior

Gate — Auth() fiber.Handler

  • Reads the Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  • Missing header / wrong scheme → 401, request stops.
  • Invalid or expired token → 401, request stops.
  • Valid token → decodes identity via utils.VerifySessionToken, stores userID + userEmail in c.Locals, calls c.Next().
  • 401 responses return a consistent JSON body { "success": false, "message": "unauthorized" } — no token internals leaked.

Companion helpers (called inside protected handlers)

  • UserID(c *fiber.Ctx) string — authenticated user's ID (or "").
  • UserEmail(c *fiber.Ctx) string — authenticated user's email (or "").

Usage

app.Get("/projects", middleware.Auth(), projectsController.List)
// inside handler:
uid := middleware.UserID(c)

Acceptance Criteria

  • Request with no Authorization header → 401
  • Request with a valid token → 200 and handler can read the user's ID
  • Request with an invalid or expired token → 401
  • User ID recovered inside the handler matches the one used to create the token (test: user-777)

Verification

  • go test ./src/middleware/ — 3 tests pass (TDD: failing first, then implemented). Tests exercise the real Fiber stack via app.Test.
  • go vet ./... — clean
  • go test ./... — ok, no failures
  • go build ./... — success

Notes for Reviewer

  • Builds directly on Task 3's VerifySessionToken; expired tokens are rejected by JWT exp validation.
  • Helpers read from c.Locals, so they only return a value after Auth() has run — safe to use in any handler wrapped by the gate.

Task 5: Request Authentication Gate

Docs

2026-06-19-floormap-backend-overview.md
2026-06-19-floormap-backend-plan.md

Context

Most of the backend's endpoints — projects, files, billing — should only be accessible to signed-in users. Rather than writing "is this user signed in?" checks inside every handler, this task creates a single reusable gate that sits in front of protected routes and automatically blocks unauthenticated requests.

Goal

Build the authentication middleware: a piece of logic that intercepts every request to a protected route, checks for a valid session token, and either lets the request through (attaching the user's identity) or rejects it with a 401 Unauthorized response.

What We're Building

  • A gate function that reads the Authorization: Bearer <token> header from every incoming request
  • If the token is missing or invalid: returns 401 immediately, request goes no further
  • If the token is valid: decodes the user's identity from it and makes that identity available to the handler downstream
  • A companion helper that handlers call to retrieve the signed-in user's details from the request

Why It Matters

Without this gate, any handler that doesn't implement its own auth check could be called by anyone. This middleware makes the protected/unprotected boundary explicit and enforced in one place. All project, file, and billing handlers depend on it.

Acceptance Criteria

  • A request with no Authorization header receives a 401 response
  • A request with a valid token receives a 200 response and the handler can read the user's ID
  • A request with an invalid or expired token receives a 401 response
  • The user's ID recovered inside the handler matches the one used to create the token

What Changes After This Task

Protected routes (projects, files, billing) can now be wrapped with this gate and trust that any request that reaches them has a verified, identified user behind it.

issues.display_title: Issue #12: Database Schema and GORM Models
issues.main_description_html:
RESULT 22/06/26

✅ Implementation Complete — Database Schema and GORM Models Ready for Unit Testing

Summary

All five tables defined as GORM models, created automatically by AutoMigrate on startup, with soft-delete, indexes, and foreign-key constraints. Verified against the live PostgreSQL instance.

DDL

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
    id             VARCHAR(36)  PRIMARY KEY,
    created_at     TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at     TIMESTAMPTZ,
    deleted_at     TIMESTAMPTZ,
    name           VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    email          VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    password       VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    email_verified BOOLEAN      DEFAULT FALSE
);
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS uni_users_email ON users (email);
CREATE INDEX        IF NOT EXISTS idx_users_deleted_at ON users (deleted_at);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS projects (
    id          VARCHAR(36)  PRIMARY KEY,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,
    deleted_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,
    user_id     VARCHAR(36)  NOT NULL,
    name        VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    status      VARCHAR(50)  DEFAULT 'draft',
    CONSTRAINT fk_projects_user FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users (id)
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_projects_user_id    ON projects (user_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_projects_deleted_at ON projects (deleted_at);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS project_files (
    id         VARCHAR(36)   PRIMARY KEY,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    project_id VARCHAR(36)   NOT NULL,
    file_name  VARCHAR(255)  NOT NULL,
    file_type  VARCHAR(100),
    file_url   VARCHAR(1024) NOT NULL,
    file_size  BIGINT        DEFAULT 0,
    CONSTRAINT fk_project_files_project FOREIGN KEY (project_id) REFERENCES projects (id)
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_project_files_project_id ON project_files (project_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_project_files_deleted_at ON project_files (deleted_at);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS otps (
    id         VARCHAR(36)  PRIMARY KEY,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    user_id    VARCHAR(36)  NOT NULL,
    token      VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    type       VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,
    expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    used_at    TIMESTAMPTZ,
    CONSTRAINT fk_otps_user FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users (id)
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_otps_user_id    ON otps (user_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_otps_token      ON otps (token);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_otps_type       ON otps (type);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_otps_deleted_at ON otps (deleted_at);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS subscriptions (
    id         VARCHAR(36)  PRIMARY KEY,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    user_id    VARCHAR(36)  NOT NULL,
    plan       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    status     VARCHAR(50)  DEFAULT 'active',
    started_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    CONSTRAINT fk_subscriptions_user FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users (id)
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_subscriptions_user_id    ON subscriptions (user_id);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_subscriptions_deleted_at ON subscriptions (deleted_at);

Deliverables

File Purpose
src/models/models.go GORM models for all five tables + AllModels()
src/config/database.go InitDB runs AutoMigrate(models.AllModels()...) on startup
docs/task/schema.sql Reference DDL (matches generated schema)
docs/schema.md Human-readable schema documentation

Tables

users, projects, project_files, otps, subscriptions — each embeds a shared Base (id VARCHAR(36) PK, created_at, updated_at, soft-delete deleted_at indexed).

Checklist

  • Define GORM models for all five tables — src/models/models.go
  • Wire up AutoMigrate on application startup — InitDB in src/config/database.go
  • Configure soft-delete (deleted_at) and indexes per table — via gorm.DeletedAt + index/uniqueIndex tags
  • Set up foreign-key relationships — associations added on User (Projects, Subscriptions, OTPs) and Project (Files); GORM generates FK constraints
  • Verify generated schema matches docs/task/schema.sql

Verification (against live PostgreSQL)

  • App tables created: otps, project_files, projects, subscriptions, users
  • Foreign keys created:
    • otps.user_id → users
    • project_files.project_id → projects
    • projects.user_id → users
    • subscriptions.user_id → users
  • docs/task/schema.sql executed in a rollback transaction — valid
  • go vet ./... clean · go test ./... ok · go build ./... success

Notes for Reviewer

  • otps consolidates the originally-planned email_verifications + password_reset_tokens into one table with a type discriminator (5 tables instead of 6) — pending approval (see Task 1).
  • Added a User.OTPs association so GORM emits the otps → users foreign key (it is not inferred without a back-reference).
  • PostGIS extension present on the database for future spatial data.

Description

Define and implement the PostgreSQL database schema for the GoThru 3D Floormap backend, matching the GORM models in src/models/models.go. GORM AutoMigrate creates these tables on startup; docs/task/schema.sql is the reference for manual setup / review.

Tables

users

  • id VARCHAR(36) PK, soft-delete timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at)
  • name, email (unique), password, email_verified (default false)
  • Indexes: unique email, deleted_at

projects

  • id PK, soft-delete timestamps, user_id FK → users(id)
  • name, description, status (default draft)
  • Indexes: user_id, deleted_at

project_files

  • id PK, soft-delete timestamps, project_id FK → projects(id)
  • file_name, file_type, file_url, file_size (default 0)
  • Indexes: project_id, deleted_at

otps

  • id PK, soft-delete timestamps, user_id FK → users(id)
  • token, type (email_verification, reset_password, ...), expires_at, used_at
  • Indexes: user_id, token, type, deleted_at

subscriptions

  • id PK, soft-delete timestamps, user_id FK → users(id)
  • plan, status (default active), started_at, expires_at
  • Indexes: user_id, deleted_at

Checklist

  • Define GORM models in src/models/models.go for all five tables.
  • Wire up AutoMigrate on application startup.
  • Configure soft-delete (deleted_at) and indexes per table.
  • Set up foreign-key relationships (projects, project_files, otps, subscriptions → users/projects).
  • Verify generated schema matches docs/task/schema.sql.

Source: docs/task/schema.sql
